question_title: What seasonally adjusted labor-force participation rate will BLS report for U.S. women ages 25–54 for March 2026?
summary: The March 2026 prime-age female LFPR (women 25-54, seasonally adjusted) will be reported in the April 2026 Employment Situation release. Starting from Feb 2026 = 78.3 (near all-time high, boosted by Jan 2026 population control revision), Monte Carlo simulations across multiple models consistently show ~65% probability of staying above 78.1. The labor market weakened significantly in Feb 2026 (-92K jobs, unemployment at 4.4%), but LFPR is sticky and doesn't respond quickly to employment shocks. The median forecast is ~78.25, with most probability mass above 78.1 due to the elevated starting point.

factors.description: Feb 2026 LFPR for women 25-54 is 78.3, near all-time high. Starting from this elevated level, inertia favors staying above 78.1 (would need 0.2+ drop in one month)
Jan 2026 population control revision shifted the series upward (78.4 revised). This structural level shift persists into March
LFPR is sticky and doesn't respond quickly to employment shocks. Prime-age women's LFPR has strong structural upward trend over recent years
Historical Feb-to-Mar changes average +0.06 with std 0.35 - wide variance but slight positive mean
US economy lost 92K jobs in Feb 2026, unemployment rose to 4.4%. Economic weakness could discourage labor force participation
DOGE federal layoffs and tariff uncertainty create ongoing downward pressure on labor market
Mean reversion risk: 78.3 is near all-time highs. Previous peak (Aug 2024: 78.4) was followed by drops to 77.7-77.8 within 3 months

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary**

| Option | Probability |
|--------|------------|
| 77.8 or below | 5% |
| 77.9 | 5% |
| 78.0 | 11% |
| 78.1 | 14% |
| Greater than 78.1 | **65%** |

**Key reasoning:**
- **Starting point is 78.3** (Feb 2026), so a drop of ≥0.2 in one month is needed to fall to 78.1 or below — historically uncommon given LFPR's stickiness
- **Population control revision** in Jan 2026 structurally shifted the series upward
- **Downside risks** from the 92K job losses in Feb, DOGE layoffs, and tariff uncertainty provide modest pressure, but LFPR typically lags employment changes by months
- **Mean reversion** from the Aug 2024 peak (78.4→77.7 in 3 months) shows sharp declines are possible but took multiple months to materialize
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What seasonally adjusted labor-force participation rate will BLS report for U.S. women ages 25–54 for March 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42633)

[Labor Force Participation Rate - 25-54 Yrs. (LNS11300060) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/LNS11300060)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the numeric value of the seasonally adjusted U.S. labor-force participation rate for women ages 25–54 for March 2026 as reported by BLS in the Employment Situation release pages at https://www.bls.gov/news.release/empsit.htm or, if needed for a cleaner direct series readout, in the corresponding FRED series page at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/LRAC25FEUSM156S
fine_print:
Use the March 2026 reported value, not a later revision. If BLS and FRED temporarily differ because of publication lag, use BLS. Candidate Sources: BLS release schedule at https://www.bls.gov/schedule/2026/03_sched.htm ; Employment Situation summary pages linked from the main BLS release.

Resolution amount will be rounded to the nearest tenth, if not reported that way by the BLS.
background_info:
This forecasts the prime-age female labor-force participation rate reported for March 2026.
